channel.title: Operational Excellence on Christa Burger
channel.description: Recent content in Operational Excellence on Christa Burger

item.title: AI Productivity Is Too Vague. Show the Burden Removed.
item.description:
“AI improves productivity” is true and also not very useful.

Productivity where?

Which burden got lighter?

What changed?

  • Did we reduce rework?
  • Did we prevent something from being forgotten?
  • Did we make decisions cleaner?
  • Did we improve evidence?
  • Did we reduce manual reconstruction?
  • Did we make the next step obvious?

That is what I care about.

Not vague productivity.

Specific relief with receipts.

This is how AI use cases should be explained.

item.title: Unnamed Work Becomes Weather
item.description:
Unnamed work becomes weather.

It just happens.

People feel it. People complain about it. People plan around it. Nobody owns it.

This is true in teams, households, operations, governance, and AI workflows.

The first act of control is often naming.

  • Name the recurring burden
  • Name the decision point
  • Name the review
  • Name the handoff
  • Name the risk
  • Name the evidence trail

Once something has a name, it can have an owner.

item.title: Accountability Should Not Require Archaeology
item.description:
If accountability requires archaeology, the system is under-designed.

People should not need to dig through inboxes, Slack threads, meeting notes, and the memory of whoever was least distracted at the time.

That is not a process.

That is a scavenger hunt with legal exposure.

AI should make this better.

Every meaningful AI-assisted workflow should be able to answer:

  • What did it do?
  • What inputs did it use?
  • What changed?
  • What did it recommend?
  • What did a human approve?
  • Where is the evidence?

This is not just a security or compliance concern.

item.title: "I Think We Talked About This" Is Not a Control
item.description:
The worst time to document a decision is after something goes wrong.

Everyone is calm. Everyone remembers clearly. No one is searching email for “final_final_ACTUAL_USE_THIS_ONE_v7.”

Obviously.

AI can help us stop doing this.

It can capture decisions as they happen:

  • What was known?
  • What was decided?
  • Who decided it?
  • What changed later?
  • What evidence existed at the time?

That is incredibly valuable.

But only if we design for it.

Otherwise AI just helps us create more outputs with less traceability.

item.title: Heroics Are Usually a Design Problem
item.description:
I used to be more impressed by heroic effort.

The late-night push. The last-minute save. The frantic deck rescue. The spreadsheet that emerges from the ashes wearing a tiny cape.

Now I mostly see heroics as a signal: a broken system relying on the goodwill of one person — or their insecurities — to keep functioning.

Sometimes heroics are necessary. Workloads can fluctuate wildly.

But that is also a lazy acceptance.
